title: The button that is not a button
checkLabels.t3: There is a real button with a word in it
checkLabels.t4: The button is inside the form
checkLabels.t5: The button knows what it does
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the form instead of fixing the button. Bring the code back.
why.untouched: You have not touched the code. The button is still fake. Change it.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
hint2.not-closed: <button> closes with </button>. Between them sits the word shown on the button.
why.lost-content: You deleted something instead of fixing it. The form has three labels, two fields, a large text box and a heading. All of that stays. Only the button changes.
hint2.lost-content: Compare with the original code. The task is one line — the one with the div.
why.no-button: There is no real button with a word in it. Either there is no <button>, or the tag is empty. An empty button shows as a small box — a blind person hears only the word button and nothing else.
hint2.no-button: The tag is <button>. The word you see sits between the opening and closing tag.
hint3.no-button: <button type="submit">Send</button> — three parts: an opening with the type, a word, a closing.
why.button-outside: The button is there, but it sits outside the form. A button outside <form> does not know which form to submit — it presses into thin air.
hint2.button-outside: The button goes INSIDE the <form>, usually at the bottom, after the last field.
why.no-type: The button is there, but it does not say what it does. Without type="submit" the form may not submit at all — older browsers differ.
hint2.no-type: type is an attribute. It lives inside the opening tag: <button type="...">.
hint3.no-type: There are three: submit sends, reset wipes, button does nothing on its own. Which one do you need here?
why.fake-button: The fake button is still there — the div labelled button. You are not adding a button next to it. You are REPLACING it.
hint2.fake-button: Delete the whole line with <div class="button">. Put a <button> in its place.
why.two-buttons: There is more than one button. A form has one way to be sent, not two.
blocks.0.text: Look at the form's button. Run it. It looks like a button. Add CSS and it will be indistinguishable from a real one.
blocks.1.text: Except it is not. It is a <div> labelled "button" — a group that means nothing.
blocks.3.text: Put the mouse away. Press Tab a few times — the cursor moves through the fields.
blocks.4.text: And then? It stops. The button is never reached. Never. Tab only visits things that take focus — fields, links, buttons. A <div> is none of them.
blocks.5.text: Try Enter while standing in a field. The form does not go. Try Space — you cannot even land on the button to press it.
blocks.6.text: Your form is fillable but unsendable — for anyone who does not use a mouse.
blocks.9.text: Change it and try again. Tab reaches it. Enter presses it. So does Space. A focus ring appears around it, so you can see where you are.
blocks.10.text: All of that is free. You wrote not one line for it.
blocks.11.text: And a warning: once the button is real, Enter will actually submit the form. The address in the action does not exist — you will get an error page. That is not broken. It is the opposite: it means the button works.
blocks.13.text: type="submit" — submits the form. It is the default inside a <form>, but write it explicitly.
blocks.14.text: type="reset" — clears everything. Do not use it. Someone will hit it by mistake instead of "send" and lose everything they wrote.
blocks.15.text: type="button" — does nothing on its own. You attach JavaScript to it later.
blocks.16.text: Why so many people get this wrong
blocks.17.text: Because a <div> is easier to style. A real button comes with built-in styles you have to strip away.
blocks.18.text: So: people build a fake button to save themselves three lines of CSS. And they pay by making the button useless to anyone without a mouse.
blocks.19.text: Three lines of CSS against all the functionality. A bad trade.
blocks.20.text: If you have to add code to make something behave like a button — it should have been a button.
blocks.22.text: The reverse mistake is just as common: an <a> that leads nowhere but does something.
blocks.23.text: The rule is simple. Does it lead somewhere — a link. Does it do something — a button.
blocks.24.text: Your turn. Make the button real.
